exemplar.title: Investigating the effects of the Halo Effect on juridical judgement.

detailedExemplarData.title: Investigating the effects of the Halo Effect on juridical judgement.

subject.title: Psychology
subject.description:
IB Psychology is a Group 3 subject in the International Baccalaureate Diploma Programme (IBDP). The course examines human behavior and mental processes through scientific inquiry, exploring biological, cognitive, and sociocultural approaches to understanding behavior. Students develop critical thinking skills while investigating contemporary psychological research, research methods, and ethical considerations in psychological practice.

The course is available at Standard Level (SL, 150 teaching hours) and Higher Level (HL, 240 hours), with first assessment in 2027.

Assessment consists of Paper 1 (integration of concepts, content, and contexts through short-answer and extended-response questions: SL 2 hours, HL 2 hours, SL 50%, HL 40%), Paper 2 (application of psychological knowledge to research contexts: SL 1 hour, HL 2 hours, SL 25%, HL 20%), Paper 3 (HL only: research methods and data analysis, 1 hour, 20%), and Internal Assessment (research proposal development: SL 25%, HL 20%).

strengths: Clearly states aim and provides a focused explanation of relevance in real-world judicial contexts
Hypothesis includes correctly operationalized IV and DV with directional and null statements
Theory description is accurate and explicitly linked to the investigation via schemaâhalo-effect mechanism
weaknesses: Minor lapses in phrasing reduce overall polish
Units of sentence length conversion (years to months) could be more clearly stated
Link between schema theory and sentencing decisions could be more tightly worded
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The introduction meets the 5â6 band descriptors by stating the aim, explaining its relevance, describing the theory and linking it to the study, and operationalizing variables.  Minor wording issues prevent full marks, so a score of 5 is justified.

strengths: Research design is well justified with counterbalancing and control of individual variability
Sampling technique and participant choice are explained with rationale
Controls and materials are described and linked to reducing data error
weaknesses: Explanation of materials and controlled variables is uneven and contains minor inaccuracies
No detailed discussion of how observation eliminated demand characteristics
Photo standardization details (lighting, background) are missing
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Although the design and sampling strands are fully explained (meeting top of the 3â4 band), the materials/variables strand is less comprehensive and includes inaccuracies.  This unevenness justifies a mid-band score of 3.

strengths: Appropriate use of Wilcoxon Signed-Rank test for nonparametric repeated measures
Graphing technique addresses the hypothesis and axes are correctly labeled
Interpretation links test results to the hypothesis
weaknesses: Contradictory descriptive statistics (identical medians vs. claimed significance)
Calculation/reporting errors in ranges and sample size inconsistencies
Graph lacks error bars or confidence intervals
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The choice of descriptive and inferential statistics and graph type is appropriate, but substantive reporting errors and misinterpretations limit accuracy.  Performance fits the 3â4 band, warranting a score of 3.

strengths: Findings are discussed with clear reference to schema theory and halo effect
Strengths and limitations are relevant, described and their impacts explained
Proposed modifications are explicitly linked to limitations and fully justified
weaknesses: Discussion occasionally repeats points without adding depth
Ethical implications (e.g., use of mugshots, consent nuances) could be explored further
Breadth of critical perspectives is slightly limited
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Evaluation meets the top-band descriptors by discussing findings with theoretical reference, explaining strengths/limitations, and fully justifying modifications.  Minor breadth and repetition issues prevent a perfect score, so 5 is awarded.

strandSelections.reasoning: A bar chart of medians for each condition is produced, which addresses the hypothesis.  Axes are labelled, but the graph shows identical bars, offering no visual evidence of the claimed effect and lacks error bars.  The technique is correct yet contains substantive interpretative weakness, placing it in the 3â4 band; a score of 3 reflects the evident errors.

The student states the Wilcoxon result, p-value and decision to accept the research hypothesis, and links the outcome back to the halo-effect prediction.  Nevertheless, the narrative fails to reconcile contradictory descriptive statistics and thus partly misinterprets the data.  Interpretation is present and tied to the hypothesis, but accuracy is compromised, warranting a 3 within the 3â4 band.

Both IV and DV are explicitly named and operationalised: IV = photographs pre-rated as attractive vs unattractive; DV = mean years (converted to months) of sentence awarded for each condition.  A directional research hypothesis and an appropriate null are written.  Everything required for the top band is present; very minor wording issues prevent full marks, so 5/6 is awarded.

Opportunity sampling is described and the choice of first-year students is explained with reference to minimising prior knowledge and repeat participation.  Issues of participant variability are also identified.  Because both the technique and its rationale are provided, the strand meets the âexplainedâ criterion; depth is solid but not exceptional, so 4/4 within the band.

The student provides an organised description of both halo effect and schema theory, cites classic and contemporary sources, and explicitly links the concepts to the planned manipulation of attractiveness.  The mechanism (social schema â halo bias â sentencing decisions) is spelled out, meeting the requirement that the theory is described AND its connection to the investigation is explained.  Some wording is informal, but conceptual accuracy is high, meriting band 5â6; a mid-band score of 5 is justified.

Findings are integrated with schema theory and halo effect and are compared to Sigall & Ostroveâs original results, demonstrating conceptual depth.  The link between cognitive theory and observed sentencing bias is explicitly discussed rather than merely described.  Minor repetition and limited critical breadth keep this at 5 rather than 6, but the descriptor for the top band is clearly met.

The student clearly states the aim â to examine whether offender attractiveness influences sentence length â and then gives a well-focused explanation of why this matters in real-world judicial contexts (possible bias, inequality and corruption).  This goes beyond merely identifying relevance; it explicitly justifies why studying the halo effect in courts is important, satisfying the descriptor for the 5â6 band.  Minor lapses in phrasing do not detract from the clarity of the aim or its explained relevance, so a mark of 5 is appropriate.

Several strengths (use of online questionnaire, first-year sample, repeated measures) and limitations (outliers, low variability, questionnaire design) are identified and each is explained in relation to its impact on validity.  The discussion remains relevant to the investigation.  Coverage is solid but not exhaustive (e.g., ethical issues lightly touched), so 5/6 is awarded.

Materials (QR code survey, six vetted mugshots, consent/debrief forms) and several controls (counterbalancing, no discussion, observation) are listed.  Some explanation of why controls/materials were chosen is given (e.g., online form prevents transcription errors), but discussion of controlled variables is thinner than for other elements and contains minor inaccuracies.  The work still meets the âexplainedâ descriptor, though less comprehensively, so a low-band 3 is appropriate.

The student proposes concrete modifications (providing average-sentence anchors, broadening age diversity) and explicitly ties each to the earlier stated limitation (uninformed guessing, low variability), providing clear justification for how the changes would enhance the study.  The linkage and rationale satisfy the âfully justifiedâ requirement; some additional procedural detail is missing, hence 5 rather than 6.

The student not only describes that a repeated-measures design was used but also justifies why it was chosen (greater data yield, control of individual sentencing leniency, avoidance of information leakage).  Limitations such as order effects are acknowledged alongside counterbalancing.  This constitutes an explanation rather than a mere description, fulfilling the 3â4 band; the coverage is thorough enough for the top of that band (4).

Both descriptive (medians, ranges) and inferential (Wilcoxon Signed-Rank Test) statistics are applied and the non-parametric choice matches a repeated-measures design with apparent non-normal data.  However, the descriptive table contradicts the claim of significant difference (identical medians), suggesting calculation or reporting errors.  Because appropriate tests are used but contain errors or misinterpretations, the 3â4 band fits; a midpoint score of 3 is awarded.

annotations.comment: The dependent variable is correctly operationalized as mean sentence length per condition, which aligns with the directional hypothesis and enhances measurement clarity.
While the operational definitions are clear, clarify whether sentence lengths provided in years were converted to months for analysis, to avoid confusion about units.
The procedural description effectively justifies using Google Forms and QR codes to minimize transcription errors and maximize accessibility, demonstrating thoughtful design.
The repeated-measures design is well justified for controlling individual variability, but you could briefly acknowledge any potential learning or fatigue effects beyond order effects.
The student offers a clear, concise definition of the halo effect, grounding the investigation in a well-established phenomenon. This provides a strong conceptual foundation for the study.
The operational definition of the independent variable is clearly stated and specific, ensuring replicability by indicating exactly how attractiveness levels were manipulated.
The median table correctly presents central tendencies, but identical medians directly contradict the claimed significant effect and warrant reconciliation.
The description of ranges miscalculates the difference (range = max â min) and uses inconsistent rounding; recalculate and report accurately.
Referencing Sigall and Ostrove for statistical analysis is misplaced; attribute the inferential procedure and p-value to the current data rather than the original study.
The description of Sigall and Ostroveâs 1975 study is thorough and relevant, effectively situating the current investigation within existing empirical work on attractiveness and judicial bias.
The link between schema theory and the halo effect is stated but could be tightened by explicitly connecting how activated social schemas lead to sentencing decisions in this specific experiment.
Opportunity sampling of first-year students is well explained with respect to prior knowledge constraints, showing a clear rationale for participant selection.
Controls such as no discussion and counterbalancing are described, but explain how participant observation ensured elimination of demand characteristics.
The materials section lists peer-rated photos and survey tools; consider adding details on photo standardization (lighting, background) to strengthen material validity.
Selection of the Wilcoxon Signed-Rank Test is appropriate for a repeated-measures design with nonparametric data, demonstrating correct statistical choice.
The value table header (âVALUE ATTRACTIVE UNATTRACTIVEâ) lacks a clear sample size (n) and treatment labels; refine the table to link directly to hypothesis testing.
The graph addresses the hypothesis and is correctly chosen, but lacks error bars or confidence intervals, limiting interpretation of variability.
Findings are thoughtfully linked to schema theory and the halo effect, demonstrating an insightful discussion of cognitive mechanisms behind sentencing bias.
The student acknowledges questionnaire design flaws and outliers, explaining their impact on validity. This limitation discussion is relevant and informative.
Linking proposed modifications directly to earlier limitations (e.g., adding sentence anchors to reduce guessing) is well justified and enhances study rigor.
Consider discussing ethical implications of using criminal mugshots and participant consent nuances to strengthen the methodological evaluation.
The primary reference to Sigall & Ostrove is correctly cited, demonstrating engagement with foundational research in halo-effect studies.
Several references lack consistent formatting (e.g., inconsistent ordering of author, date, title). Adopt a single style (APA/MLA) for coherence.
Some URLs are provided without retrieval dates or standardized formats, hindering traceability; include access dates or DOIs where possible.
The descriptive summary notes identical medians but the Wilcoxon test uses n=80, conflicting with the reported sample of 32. Reconcile sampleâsize inconsistencies.
